title: Brik
subtitle: Tunisian fried pastry with egg and tuna
excerpt: A thin round of pastry folded over a filling of tuna, onion, capers, and a whole egg, then fried in hot oil until the pastry is blistered and golden and the egg yolk is still runny. The goal is to bite through the crisp pastry and release the liquid yolk without spilling it. Tunisia's most famous street food.
sourceNotes: Brik is the Tunisian adaptation of the Ottoman börek tradition, using the thin North African pastry called malsouka or warka (equivalent to Turkish yufka) rather than filo. The tuna and egg filling is the standard version; variations include potato, cheese, or ground meat. Brik is eaten throughout Tunisia and in the Tunisian diaspora in France.

content.text: Malsouka (the traditional Tunisian pastry sheet) is sold fresh in Tunisian shops in France and Britain. Filo pastry is a workable substitute: use a single sheet or two overlapping sheets folded into a round. The pastry should be thin enough that the frying oil creates visible bubbles across its surface.

The runny egg yolk is the point of brik. The oil must be at the right temperature: 180°C. Too hot and the egg white sets before the pastry crisps; too cool and the pastry absorbs oil and the egg overcooks. Fry one at a time, hold it half-submerged to seal the open edge before full submersion, and lift out as soon as the pastry is golden.

items.name: Filo pastry
items.prepNote: sheets, or use malsouka if available

content.text: Mix the {{tuna-tinned}}, {{onion}}, {{capers}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{harissa}} (if using), {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}} in a bowl. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep, wide pan to 180°C. Lay a sheet of {{filo-pastry}} flat. Place a quarter of the tuna filling on one half of the pastry circle, leaving a 3 cm border around the edge. Make a well in the filling and crack one {{eggs}} into it. Fold the empty half of the pastry over the filling to form a half-moon, pressing the edges to seal. Lower the brik into the oil. Fry for 2 to 3 minutes, turning once, until golden and crisp. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve immediately with lemon wedges.

items.fix: Make the well in the filling deep enough that the egg white is in contact with the pastry. The white cooks from conducted heat through the pastry
items.cause: Too much filling over the egg, blocking heat from the white
items.symptom: Egg white not fully set but pastry already done

items.fix: The oil must be at 180°C before the brik goes in. Too cool and the pastry absorbs oil rather than crisping. Use a thermometer or test with a small piece of pastry. It should sizzle immediately
Press the pastry edge firmly and fold it over itself if possible. Work quickly once the egg is in. It will start to cook from the heat of the filling
items.symptom: Brik breaks open in the oil

content.text: Brik is eaten throughout Tunisia at all hours: as a street snack, a first course, or a fast meal. The drama of eating brik (the attempt to keep the yolk intact while biting through the crisp pastry) is part of the social experience. Variations include brik bil kefta (minced meat), brik bil batata (potato and egg, without tuna), and brik with cheese. In France, where Tunisian immigration is significant, brik appears in North African restaurants and in the home kitchens of the diaspora. Outside North Africa, the egg-in-crispy-pastry combination is unusual enough to surprise and impress.
